subs.parent_policy_name: Strengthen Meaningful Community Engagement and Public Education and Build Adaptive Capacity across all Sectors (AR3).
subs.extraction_rationale: Program establishment commitment with specific audience and purpose.

subs.policy_statement: Establish a residential and small business resilience audit program (NYSERDA with DEC, HCR, OTDA, DHSES consultation).
subs.verbatim_text: - Establish a resilience audit program: NYSERDA, in consultation with DEC, New York State Homes and Community Renewal (HCR), New York State Office of Temporary and Disability Assistance (OTDA), and the New York State Division of Homeland Security and Emergency Services (DHSES) should establish a residential and small business resilience audit program.
subs.parent_policy_name: Strengthen Meaningful Community Engagement and Public Education and Build Adaptive Capacity across all Sectors (AR3).
subs.extraction_rationale: Specific new program creation with defined scope (residential and small business).

section_header: ar4. identify and evaluate options for supporting equitable adaptation and resilience practices and projects, and to enhance insurance protection

parent.policy_statement: Identify and Evaluate Options for Supporting Equitable Adaptation and Resilience Practices and Projects, and to Enhance Insurance Protection (AR4).
parent.verbatim_text: ## AR4.  Identify and Evaluate Options for Supporting Equitable Adaptation and Resilience Practices and Projects, and to Enhance Insurance Protection
parent.section_header: AR4.  Identify and Evaluate Options for Supporting Equitable Adaptation and Resilience Practices and Projects, and to Enhance Insurance Protection
parent.extraction_rationale: Named strategy with multiple finance/insurance components.

subs.policy_statement: Create a resilient infrastructure fund through bonding and prioritize investments in Disadvantaged Communities, exploring equitable benefit-cost analysis methods.
subs.verbatim_text: - Create a resilient infrastructure fund and prioritize investments in Disadvantaged Communities: The State should create a resilient infrastructure fund through bonding, such as through the 'Clean Air, Clean Water, and Green Jobs' Environmental Bond Act. In developing investment programs, the State should explore creation of equitable methods for benefit-cost analysis and valuing damages and losses.
subs.parent_policy_name: Identify and Evaluate Options for Supporting Equitable Adaptation and Resilience Practices and Projects, and to Enhance Insurance Protection (AR4).
subs.extraction_rationale: Resource allocation mechanism (bonding fund) with DAC prioritization.

subs.policy_statement: Impose an insurance-premium surcharge for select lines on high-value, high-risk properties to support risk-reduction and adaptation projects.
subs.verbatim_text: - Establish an insurance-premium surcharge for high-value, high-risk properties: Impose a surcharge on insurance premiums for select lines of insurance to support risk-reduction and adaptation projects.
subs.parent_policy_name: Identify and Evaluate Options for Supporting Equitable Adaptation and Resilience Practices and Projects, and to Enhance Insurance Protection (AR4).
subs.extraction_rationale: Specific financing policy instrument (premium surcharge) with stated use.

subs.policy_statement: Enact legislation authorizing all municipalities to establish community preservation funds.
subs.verbatim_text: - Authorize community preservation funds for all municipalities: The State should enact legislation authorizing all municipalities to establish community preservation funds.
subs.parent_policy_name: Identify and Evaluate Options for Supporting Equitable Adaptation and Resilience Practices and Projects, and to Enhance Insurance Protection (AR4).
subs.extraction_rationale: Clear legislative action authorizing a local funding tool.

subs.policy_statement: Encourage anchor institutions to direct community benefit investments toward equitable climate change projects and local wealth building (DOH).
subs.verbatim_text: - Focus anchor-institution investment on community benefit and wealth building: New York Department of Health (DOH) should encourage anchor institutions ... to focus community benefit investments on projects to equitably address climate change and build local community wealth.
subs.parent_policy_name: Identify and Evaluate Options for Supporting Equitable Adaptation and Resilience Practices and Projects, and to Enhance Insurance Protection (AR4).
subs.extraction_rationale: Specific directive to DOH to influence investment behavior toward climate/equity outcomes.

subs.policy_statement: Report on options to enhance hazard mitigation funding, prefund disaster recovery, and transfer catastrophic risk to insurance/capital markets (Division of Budget or appropriate agency).
subs.verbatim_text: - Explore hazard mitigation funding alternatives: The Division of Budget, or other appropriate agency, should report on options to enhance hazard mitigation funding and to prefund disaster recovery, and to transfer catastrophic risk to the insurance and capital markets.
subs.parent_policy_name: Identify and Evaluate Options for Supporting Equitable Adaptation and Resilience Practices and Projects, and to Enhance Insurance Protection (AR4).
subs.extraction_rationale: Mandates a formal report exploring specific financing/risk-transfer options.

subs.policy_statement: Implement strategies to increase take-up rates of flood insurance and other climate hazard coverage (DEC and partners).
subs.verbatim_text: - Improve insurance coverage: DEC and partners at all levels of government should implement strategies to increase take-up rates of flood insurance and other coverage related to climate hazards.
subs.parent_policy_name: Identify and Evaluate Options for Supporting Equitable Adaptation and Resilience Practices and Projects, and to Enhance Insurance Protection (AR4).
subs.extraction_rationale: Specific implementation commitment to raise insurance participation.

subs.policy_statement: Adopt legislation to prohibit or restrict anti-concurrent causation clauses for sewer backup insurance where flooding is the cause.
subs.verbatim_text: - Restrict anti-concurrent causation clauses: The State should adopt legislation to prohibit or restrict anti-concurrent causation clauses for sewer backup insurance coverage where flooding is the cause.
subs.parent_policy_name: Identify and Evaluate Options for Supporting Equitable Adaptation and Resilience Practices and Projects, and to Enhance Insurance Protection (AR4).
subs.extraction_rationale: Concrete legislative restriction targeting insurance contract terms.

section_header: ar5. provide state agency planning and technical support for equitable regional and local adaptation and resilience plans and projects

parent.policy_statement: Provide State Agency Planning and Technical Support for Equitable Regional and Local Adaptation and Resilience Plans and Projects (AR5).
parent.verbatim_text: ## AR5.  Provide State Agency Planning and Technical Support for Equitable Regional and Local Adaptation and Resilience Plans and Projects
parent.section_header: AR5.  Provide State Agency Planning and Technical Support for Equitable Regional and Local Adaptation and Resilience Plans and Projects
parent.extraction_rationale: Named strategy with multiple support actions listed.

subs.policy_statement: Expand programs to support local resilience, continuity, and adaptive capacity and consideration of climate change in local regulatory and planning programs (DEC, DOS, others).
subs.verbatim_text: - Develop local adaptation capacity: DEC, DOS, and other agencies should expand programs to support development of local resilience, continuity and adaptive capacity and consideration of climate change in local regulatory and planning programs.
subs.parent_policy_name: Provide State Agency Planning and Technical Support for Equitable Regional and Local Adaptation and Resilience Plans and Projects (AR5).

subs.policy_statement: Support development of local economic resilience strategies and climate-adapted economic development and continuity planning (DOS, ESD, others).

subs.parent_policy_name: Provide State Agency Planning and Technical Support for Equitable Regional and Local Adaptation and Resilience Plans and Projects (AR5).
subs.extraction_rationale: Concrete support action spanning planning/finance/budgeting.

subs.policy_statement: Support deployment of multilingual guidance documents and online tools for vulnerability assessments, adaptation planning, and implementation (DEC with partner agencies).
subs.verbatim_text:
- Deploy online tools: DEC and partner agencies, including DOS, NYSERDA, DHSES, and the Office of Information Technology Services, should support deployment of online tools to

facilitate vulnerability assessments, adaptation planning and implementation. As feasible, guidance documents and online tools should be made available in several languages.
subs.parent_policy_name: Provide State Agency Planning and Technical Support for Equitable Regional and Local Adaptation and Resilience Plans and Projects (AR5).
subs.extraction_rationale: Specific tool deployment commitment including language accessibility.

subs.policy_statement: Support community-led pre-event, long-term recovery planning, including consideration of managed retreat from highly vulnerable areas (DOS and DEC).
subs.verbatim_text: - Support recovery planning: DOS and DEC should support community-led pre-event, long-term recovery planning. Such planning should include consideration of managed retreat from highly vulnerable areas.
subs.parent_policy_name: Provide State Agency Planning and Technical Support for Equitable Regional and Local Adaptation and Resilience Plans and Projects (AR5).
subs.extraction_rationale: Concrete planning support action with explicit managed retreat consideration.

subs.policy_statement: Analyze relocation and buyout of properties as potential alternatives to electrification of at-risk buildings (NYSERDA with DEC, HCR, DOS).
subs.verbatim_text: - Consider relocation and buyouts as alternatives to electrification: NYSERDA, in consultation with DEC, HCR, and DOS, should analyze relocation and buyout of properties as potential alternatives to electrification of at-risk buildings.
subs.parent_policy_name: Provide State Agency Planning and Technical Support for Equitable Regional and Local Adaptation and Resilience Plans and Projects (AR5).
subs.extraction_rationale: Specific analytical task to evaluate policy alternatives for at-risk buildings.

subs.policy_statement: Establish strike teams to equitably assist municipalities with resilient post-disaster recovery (CSRO).
subs.verbatim_text: - Establish post-disaster strike teams: The CSRO should establish strike teams to equitably assist municipalities with resilient post-disaster recovery.
subs.parent_policy_name: Provide State Agency Planning and Technical Support for Equitable Regional and Local Adaptation and Resilience Plans and Projects (AR5).
subs.extraction_rationale: Concrete operational support mechanism (strike teams) with equity goal.

subs.policy_statement: Convene a work group to develop a State strategy to address climate migration, including differential effects of relocation strategies in Disadvantaged Communities.
subs.verbatim_text: - Plan for climate migration: The State should convene a work group, to include DEC, NYSERDA, DOS, HCR, DHSES, Governor's Office of Storm Recovery, subject experts from State University of New York (SUNY) or other universities, and refugee resettlement agencies, to develop a strategy to address climate migration, including consideration of differential effects of relocation strategies in Disadvantaged Communities.
subs.parent_policy_name: Provide State Agency Planning and Technical Support for Equitable Regional and Local Adaptation and Resilience Plans and Projects (AR5).
subs.extraction_rationale: Specific governance action to create a strategy on climate migration with equity lens.

parent.section_header: AR6.  Evaluate Opportunities to Ensure Equitable Consideration of Future Climate Conditions in Land Use Planning and Environmental Reviews

subs.parent_policy_name: Evaluate Opportunities to Ensure Equitable Consideration of Future Climate Conditions in Land Use Planning and Environmental Reviews (AR6).

subs.policy_statement: Amend the project review process to facilitate approval of climate adaptation projects (DEC).

subs.policy_statement: Enact legislation to include climate mitigation, adaptation/resilience, and biodiversity as potential topics in comprehensive plans.
subs.verbatim_text: - Consider climate and biodiversity in comprehensive plans: The State should enact legislation amending relevant statutes to include consideration of climate mitigation, adaptation and resilience, and biodiversity as potential topics in comprehensive plans.
subs.parent_policy_name: Evaluate Opportunities to Ensure Equitable Consideration of Future Climate Conditions in Land Use Planning and Environmental Reviews (AR6).
subs.extraction_rationale: Explicit legislative amendment to planning statutes.

subs.policy_statement: Require consideration of forest and farmland protection in all comprehensive and other land use plans undertaken or funded by the State.
subs.verbatim_text: - Protect forests and farmland: The State should require consideration of forest and farmland protection in all comprehensive and other land use plans that it undertakes or funds.
subs.parent_policy_name: Evaluate Opportunities to Ensure Equitable Consideration of Future Climate Conditions in Land Use Planning and Environmental Reviews (AR6).
subs.extraction_rationale: Clear statewide requirement applied to state-funded/undertaken plans.

subs.parent_policy_name: Develop Policies, Programs, and Decision Support Tools to Reduce Risks Associated with Coastal and Inland Flooding (AR7).

subs.policy_statement: Support dam removals that reduce flood risk and improve aquatic habitat quality (DEC).

section_header: ar8. develop policies and programs to reduce human risks associated with new patterns of thermal extremes

parent.policy_statement: Develop Policies and Programs to Reduce Human Risks Associated with New Patterns of Thermal Extremes (AR8).
parent.verbatim_text: ## AR8.  Develop Policies and Programs to Reduce Human Risks Associated with New Patterns of Thermal Extremes
parent.section_header: AR8.  Develop Policies and Programs to Reduce Human Risks Associated with New Patterns of Thermal Extremes

subs.verbatim_text: - Implement the extreme heat action plan: DEC and NYSERDA should coordinate with the EHAPWG to ensure the equitable implementation of all provisions of the extreme heat action plan. The EHAPWG should create a mechanism for evaluating implementation progress and tracking extreme heat related health outcomes and regularly update the extreme heat action plan.
subs.parent_policy_name: Develop Policies and Programs to Reduce Human Risks Associated with New Patterns of Thermal Extremes (AR8).

subs.policy_statement: Support development and operation of cooling centers and assess accessibility via public transportation (DEC/DOH with EHAPWG).

subs.policy_statement: Support development of regional and local heat emergency plans prioritizing vulnerable communities and facilitate incorporation into hazard mitigation plans (DOH/DHSES).
subs.verbatim_text: - Develop regional and local heat emergency plans: DOH should support development of regional and local heat emergency plans that prioritize the health and stability of vulnerable communities. DHSES should facilitate incorporation of these heat emergency plans into county and municipal hazard mitigation plans.
subs.parent_policy_name: Develop Policies and Programs to Reduce Human Risks Associated with New Patterns of Thermal Extremes (AR8).
subs.extraction_rationale: Defines specific planning products and integration into existing mitigation planning.

subs.policy_statement: Amend the State building code to require more effective weatherization for thermal extremes (DOS).
subs.verbatim_text: - Strengthen weatherization requirements: DOS should amend the State building code to require more effective weatherization from thermal extremes.
subs.parent_policy_name: Develop Policies and Programs to Reduce Human Risks Associated with New Patterns of Thermal Extremes (AR8).
subs.extraction_rationale: Concrete code amendment requirement tied to climate hazard (thermal extremes).

subs.policy_statement: Promote and facilitate access to programs providing cooling, weatherization, and solar assistance to vulnerable populations (OTDA/NYSERDA).
subs.verbatim_text: - Enhance thermal resilience in vulnerable populations: OTDA and NYSERDA should promote and facilitate access to programs that provide cooling, weatherization, and solar assistance to vulnerable populations.
subs.parent_policy_name: Develop Policies and Programs to Reduce Human Risks Associated with New Patterns of Thermal Extremes (AR8).
subs.extraction_rationale: Specific access-promotion action targeted to vulnerable populations.

subs.policy_statement: Develop a comprehensive outreach and communications strategy to reduce extreme heat risks, including assessing and addressing deficiencies in advisories and internal/external communications (DOH with EHAPWG).
subs.verbatim_text: - Develop and implement a comprehensive outreach and communications strategy to reduce risks of extreme heat: DOH, in coordination with the EHAPWG, should develop a comprehensive strategy for internal and external communications that reduce extreme heat risks, including assessing adequacy of, and addressing deficiencies in, current advisories, agency/authority internal communications, and other internal and external outreach and public education initiatives.
subs.parent_policy_name: Develop Policies and Programs to Reduce Human Risks Associated with New Patterns of Thermal Extremes (AR8).
subs.extraction_rationale: Specific strategy deliverable and assessment/improvement requirements.

subs.policy_statement: Develop a strategy to promote and incentivize green infrastructure and natural resources (including urban forests) to reduce climate risks, and identify urban heat island locations in Disadvantaged Communities (DEC and others).
subs.verbatim_text: - Adopt a green infrastructure plan: DEC and other relevant agencies should develop a strategy to promote and incentivize use of green infrastructure and natural resources, including urban forests, to reduce climate risks. In support of this planning, DEC will identify locations of urban heat islands in Disadvantaged Communities.
subs.parent_policy_name: Develop Policies and Programs to Reduce Human Risks Associated with New Patterns of Thermal Extremes (AR8).

section_header: ar9. ensure the reliability, resilience, and safety of the energy system

parent.policy_statement: Ensure the Reliability, Resilience, and Safety of the Energy System (AR9).
parent.verbatim_text: ## AR9.  Ensure the Reliability, Resilience, and Safety of the Energy System
parent.section_header: AR9.  Ensure the Reliability, Resilience, and Safety of the Energy System
parent.extraction_rationale: Named strategy with specific regulatory/planning components for energy resilience.

subs.policy_statement: Require regulated utilities and critical infrastructure operators to incorporate updated climate projections into required vulnerability assessments as updated and periodically review resilience standards for incorporation into PSC-approved risk-reduction plans; support non-PSC utilities in developing their own requirements/plans (PSC).
subs.verbatim_text:
The PSC should require regulated utilities and operators of critical infrastructure to incorporate updated climate change projections into required

vulnerability assessments as the assessments are updated and should periodically review established resilience standards and vulnerabilities to climate hazards to ensure their incorporation into PSC-approved risk-reduction plans. Municipal utilities, cooperative utilities, Long Island Power Authority (LIPA), New York Power Authority (NYPA), and other utilities not regulated by the PSC should be supported in the development of their own requirements and plans.
subs.parent_policy_name: Ensure the Reliability, Resilience, and Safety of the Energy System (AR9).
subs.extraction_rationale: Regulatory requirement and periodic review commitment with explicit scope.

subs.policy_statement: Convene a work group to develop strategies ensuring fuel/power availability for emergency fleets and essential transit during grid outages and to establish an EV-charging infrastructure resilience plan (CSRO or designee).
subs.verbatim_text: - Develop strategies for grid outages and extreme weather events: The CSRO or other designated individual should convene a work group... to develop strategies to ensure availability of fuel and power for emergency vehicular fleet operations and essential public transportation during power grid outages. This work group should also establish a resilience plan for EV-charging infrastructure to ensure access to transportation, including evacuation during extreme weather events.
subs.parent_policy_name: Ensure the Reliability, Resilience, and Safety of the Energy System (AR9).
subs.extraction_rationale: Specific governance mechanism (work group) producing strategies and a resilience plan.

subs.policy_statement: Promote capital improvements in buildings to endure grid failures and facilitate safe re-energization (NYSERDA).
subs.verbatim_text: - Promote capital improvements: NYSERDA, in consultation with DPS, DOS, and other relevant entities, should promote capital improvements in buildings to endure grid failures and to facilitate buildings' ability to accept power when the system is re-energized.
subs.parent_policy_name: Ensure the Reliability, Resilience, and Safety of the Energy System (AR9).
subs.extraction_rationale: Concrete promotion action targeting building resilience to outages.

subs.policy_statement: Include photovoltaic (PV) and electric vehicle (EV) charging readiness requirements in the building code (DOS with NYSERDA consultation).
subs.verbatim_text: - Include photovoltaic (PV) and electric vehicle (EV) charging in building code: DOS, in consultation with NYSERDA, should include requirements for PV and EV charging readiness in the building code.
subs.parent_policy_name: Ensure the Reliability, Resilience, and Safety of the Energy System (AR9).
subs.extraction_rationale: Explicit building code requirement affecting PV/EV readiness.

subs.policy_statement: Develop a comprehensive strategy to support islandable microgrids and district systems using renewable energy and behind-the-meter storage, especially at critical facilities, for grid emergencies (NYSERDA).
subs.verbatim_text: - Support local renewable and storage systems: NYSERDA, in consultation with DPS, DHSES, and local governments, should develop a comprehensive strategy to support development of islandable microgrids and district systems using renewable sources of energy to provide locally generated power and behind-the-meter storage, especially in critical facilities, for use during grid emergencies.
subs.parent_policy_name: Ensure the Reliability, Resilience, and Safety of the Energy System (AR9).
subs.extraction_rationale: Strategy development commitment specifying technologies (microgrids, storage) and priority sites.

section_header: chapter 1. executive summary / 1.1 climate action

individual.policy_statement: Adopt and implement New York State's Climate Leadership and Community Protection Act (Climate Act) emission reduction requirements: reduce economywide GHG emissions 40% by 2030 and 85% by 2050 from 1990 levels, and achieve net zero emissions statewide by 2050.
individual.verbatim_text:
This Scoping Plan includes recommendations to meet the Climate Act's nation-leading goals and requirements, including actions to achieve a reduction in economywide greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions of 40% by 2030 and 85% by 2050 from 1990 levels, which will put New York on a path toward carbon neutrality...

It identifies actions needed for New York to achieve... a 40% reduction in statewide GHG emissions from 1990 levels by 2030, an 85% reduction in statewide GHG emissions from 1990 levels by 2050, and net zero emissions statewide by 2050.
individual.section_header: Chapter 1. Executive Summary / 1.1 Climate Action
individual.extraction_rationale: Statutory, quantified statewide emissions targets with deadlines; clearly a binding policy requirement under the Climate Act.

section_header: 1.1 climate action / electricity

individual.policy_statement: Meet the Climate Act electricity mandates: 70% renewable electricity by 2030 and 100% zero-emission electricity by 2040.
individual.verbatim_text:
It identifies actions needed for New York to achieve 70% renewable electricity by 2030, 100% zero-emission electricity by 2040...

The Climate Act requires that 70% of statewide electricity come from renewable energy sources by 2030 (70x30) and that the State achieve a zero-emission electricity system by 2040 (100x40).
individual.section_header: 1.1 Climate Action / Electricity
individual.extraction_rationale: Quantified, dated clean electricity requirements explicitly stated as Climate Act requirements.

section_header: electricity

individual.policy_statement: Meet the Climate Act clean energy deployment requirements: install 6,000 MW distributed solar by 2025, procure/deploy 3,000 MW energy storage by 2030, and procure 9,000 MW offshore wind by 2035.
individual.verbatim_text: It also requires that the State install 6,000 megawatts (MW) of distributed solar by 2025, 3,000 MW of energy storage by 2030, and 9,000 MW of offshore wind by 2035.
individual.section_header: Electricity
individual.extraction_rationale: Specific capacity targets with deadlines; clearly policy requirements under the Climate Act.

section_header: 1.5 next steps

individual.policy_statement: DEC must draft and promulgate enforceable regulations by January 1, 2024 to ensure New York meets the statewide GHG emission limits (40% by 2030 and 85% by 2050 from 1990 levels).
individual.verbatim_text: DEC will have until January 1, 2024, to draft and promulgate enforceable regulations to ensure that the State meets the Climate Act's statewide GHG emission limits (i.e., 40% reduction in Statewide GHG emissions by 2030 and 85% reduction by 2050, both from 1990 levels) as outlined in the Scoping Plan.
individual.section_header: 1.5 Next Steps
individual.extraction_rationale: Time-bound regulatory mandate with explicit deadline and purpose.

section_header: 1.5 next steps

individual.policy_statement: PSC must issue a comprehensive review of the renewable energy program by July 1, 2024 and every two years thereafter, covering progress toward 70% renewable electricity by 2030 and 100% zero-emission electricity by 2040 and including program progress for 9 GW offshore wind (2035), 6 GW solar PV (2025), and 3 GW energy storage (2030).
individual.verbatim_text: By July 1, 2024, and every two years thereafter, the PSC will issue a comprehensive review of the renewable energy program, including progress in meeting the overall targets for 70% renewable electricity by 2030 and 100% zero-emission electricity by 2040. This review will also include a progress update on the programs the PSC has established to require procurement of 9 GW of offshore wind by 2035, 6 GW of solar PV by 2025, and 3 GW of energy storage by 2030.
individual.section_header: 1.5 Next Steps
individual.extraction_rationale: Clear procedural requirement with recurring schedule and defined scope.

section_header: economywide cap-and-invest program

individual.policy_statement: Implement an economywide cap-and-invest program to ensure Climate Act emission limits are met and to generate revenues for investments (including benefits for Disadvantaged Communities) while addressing leakage risk; offsets to have little or no role; include rebates/other mechanisms to mitigate impacts on low- and moderate-income households.
individual.verbatim_text:
The Scoping Plan recommends implementation of an economywide cap-and-invest program that would ensure the Climate Act's emission limits are met while providing support for clean technology market development.

Offsets would have little, if any, role in a cap-and-invest program designed to comply with the Climate Act.

In addition, the strategy recommends rebates or other mechanisms to mitigate the program's financial impacts on LMI households...
individual.section_header: Economywide Cap-and-Invest Program
individual.extraction_rationale: Named program recommendation with defined mechanism (cap-and-invest), design constraints (offsets limited), and specified equity/consumer provisions.

section_header: buildings — adopt zero-emission codes and standards and require energy benchmarking for buildings

individual.policy_statement: Adopt advanced State building codes requiring new buildings to be built to a zero-emission and highly efficient standard without fossil-fuel combustion equipment starting in 2025 for low-rise residential new construction and 2028 for commercial new construction, including incorporating strategies for building resilience.
individual.verbatim_text: Advanced State codes is a key strategy for requiring residential and commercial buildings to be built to a zeroemission and highly efficient standard (without equipment used for the combustion of fossil fuels) starting in 2025 for low-rise residential new construction and in 2028 for commercial new construction, and for incorporating strategies for building resilience.
individual.section_header: Buildings — Adopt Zero-Emission Codes and Standards and Require Energy Benchmarking for Buildings
individual.extraction_rationale: Concrete regulatory action with start dates and clear performance requirement (zero-emission, no fossil combustion equipment).

section_header: buildings — adopt zero-emission codes and standards and require energy benchmarking for buildings

individual.policy_statement: Adopt equipment standards requiring buildings to transition to modern technologies such as heat pumps when equipment is replaced to achieve needed emissions reductions in the buildings sector.
individual.verbatim_text: This Scoping Plan includes recommendations for the adoption of equipment standards that require buildings to transition to modern technologies such as heat pumps that achieve the needed emissions reductions in the sector when equipment is replaced.
individual.section_header: Buildings — Adopt Zero-Emission Codes and Standards and Require Energy Benchmarking for Buildings
individual.extraction_rationale: Specific recommended standards tied to a trigger (equipment replacement) and a named technology (heat pumps).

section_header: buildings — adopt zero-emission codes and standards and require energy benchmarking for buildings

individual.policy_statement: Implement energy benchmarking with disclosure requirements and building performance standards to encourage efficient operation and capital investments in high-performance envelopes and efficient HVAC systems.
individual.verbatim_text: In addition, energy benchmarking with disclosure requirements and building performance standards will encourage efficient operation of buildings and capital investments in high-performance building envelopes and efficient heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC) systems.
individual.section_header: Buildings — Adopt Zero-Emission Codes and Standards and Require Energy Benchmarking for Buildings
individual.extraction_rationale: Concrete policy instruments (benchmarking, disclosure, performance standards) with stated purpose.

section_header: buildings — scale up public financial incentives and expand access to public and private low-cost financing for building decarbonization

individual.policy_statement: Scale up public funding and low-cost financing for building decarbonization, including strategic funding to accelerate market adoption of weatherization, electrification, energy efficiency and resiliency upgrades, expanded dedicated support for low- and moderate-income households/affordable and public housing/Disadvantaged Communities, and support for thermal energy networks and workforce transition.
individual.verbatim_text: Public funding should be scaled up and used strategically to accelerate wide market adoption of weatherization, electrification, and additional energy efficiency and resiliency upgrades; to expand dedicated financial support for LMI households, affordable and public housing, and Disadvantaged Communities... and to promote thermal energy networks with support for transitioning the existing workforce and workforce development.
individual.section_header: Buildings — Scale Up Public Financial Incentives and Expand Access to Public and Private Low-Cost Financing for Building Decarbonization
individual.extraction_rationale: Explicit recommendation to scale funding/financing with defined beneficiary groups and covered measures.

section_header: buildings — transition from hydrofluorocarbons

individual.policy_statement: Continue adopting regulations and coordinating with other states to reduce hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs), and support the transition via technical resources/toolkits, workforce training, demonstration projects, and incentives for low-GWP refrigerant technologies (including natural refrigerants).
individual.verbatim_text: New York State agencies should continue to adopt regulations and coordinate with other states on HFC reduction policies to ensure an effective phase-down of HFCs. For this transition, the State should support technical resources and toolkits, workforce training, demonstration projects, incentives that make low-global warming potential refrigerant technologies and alternatives available and affordable, including a focus on natural refrigerants.
individual.section_header: Buildings — Transition from Hydrofluorocarbons
individual.extraction_rationale: Clear action commitments (regulations, coordination, incentives/training) tied to a specific pollutant group (HFCs).

section_header: industry — establish ghg emissions registry and reporting system

individual.policy_statement: Establish (or expand) a greenhouse gas emissions registry and reporting system to improve facility-level emissions data and support more focused industrial emissions reductions.
individual.verbatim_text: In order to ensure the State has sufficient emissions data, it should establish a new GHG emissions registry and reporting system or expand existing GHG emissions reporting requirements.
individual.section_header: Industry — Establish GHG Emissions Registry and Reporting System
individual.extraction_rationale: Specific intervention (registry/reporting system) proposed to address an identified data need.

section_header: 1.2 climate justice / directing benefits to disadvantaged communities

individual.policy_statement: Prioritize Disadvantaged Communities by directing at least 35% (goal 40%) of benefits of spending on clean energy and energy efficiency programs/projects/investments to Disadvantaged Communities, and develop a methodology (with CJWG consultation) for defining and tracking those benefits across agencies.
individual.verbatim_text:
The Climate Act requires that Disadvantaged Communities receive a minimum of 35%, with a goal of 40%, of the benefits of spending on clean energy and energy efficiency programs, projects, or investments...

State agencies, in consultation with the CJWG and other relevant stakeholders, are developing a methodology for defining the benefits of State investments in Disadvantaged Communities.
individual.section_header: 1.2 Climate Justice / Directing Benefits to Disadvantaged Communities
individual.extraction_rationale: Quantified statutory minimum/goal plus a defined administrative action (methodology development) to implement it.

section_header: community air monitoring in disadvantaged communities

individual.policy_statement: Implement the Climate Act statewide community air monitoring program in Disadvantaged Communities: conduct monitoring in at least four highest-priority Disadvantaged Communities; identify and monitor 10 disproportionately burdened communities; complete monitoring by Q3 2023; provide quarterly updates; and prepare by June 1, 2024 strategies to reduce toxic and criteria air pollutants in Disadvantaged Communities, leading to community emissions reduction programs.
individual.verbatim_text:
The Climate Act requires that DEC conduct this Statewide Community Air Monitoring program in no less than four Disadvantaged Communities that have been identified as the highest priority due to high air pollution exposure burdens.

Exceeding the four required by the Climate Act, 10 communities were identified as having a disproportionate air pollution burden...

The statewide community air monitoring program is scheduled to be completed in the third quarter of 2023. DEC will provide quarterly project updates...

By June 1, 2024, DEC will prepare... strategies to reduce emissions of toxic and criteria air pollutants in Disadvantaged Communities.
individual.section_header: Community Air Monitoring in Disadvantaged Communities
individual.extraction_rationale: Concrete program requirements with minimum coverage, expanded coverage, deadlines, reporting commitments, and follow-on strategy requirement.

section_header: community air monitoring in disadvantaged communities

individual.policy_statement: Provide additional funding for community-led air monitoring: $2 million in grants for community-led complementary monitoring and $1 million in Community Air Monitoring Capacity Building Grants.
individual.verbatim_text: The air monitoring effort will be bolstered by an additional $2 million in grants that will support community-led, complementary air monitoring efforts, and $1 million in Community Air Monitoring Capacity Building Grants.
individual.section_header: Community Air Monitoring in Disadvantaged Communities
individual.extraction_rationale: Explicit resource allocation amounts tied to a defined program purpose.

section_header: 6.3 barriers and opportunities report

individual.policy_statement: Require State entities to conduct a self-assessment and barriers analysis (guided by the Barriers and Opportunities Report) and translate results into a plan with workstreams, timelines, and expected outcomes to address barriers to serving Disadvantaged Communities.
individual.verbatim_text:
The Barriers and Opportunities Report recommends that State agencies and authorities conduct a self-assessment and barriers analysis related to their policies and programs guided by the findings in the Report.

In conducting the self-assessment and barriers analysis, each State entity should be expected to... Translate the results into a plan that identifies the relevant barriers and opportunities, specific workstreams... a timeline for each workstream, and the expected outcome of each workstream
individual.section_header: 6.3 Barriers and Opportunities Report
individual.extraction_rationale: Describes an expected, structured administrative action by agencies with specified deliverables.

section_header: 3.2 overview of new york's climate policies — regulatory action

individual.policy_statement: Governor-directed policy: DEC must adopt regulations requiring all new passenger cars, pickup trucks, and SUVs sold in New York State to be zero-emission by 2035, modeled on California's Advanced Clean Cars II.
individual.verbatim_text: Governor Hochul directed DEC to adopt regulations that will require all new passenger cars, pickup trucks, and SUVs sold in New York State be zero-emission by 2035 and be modeled on California's Advanced Clean Cars II regulation finalized in 2022.
individual.section_header: 3.2 Overview of New York's Climate Policies — Regulatory Action
individual.extraction_rationale: Regulatory directive with a clear end-date requirement and defined regulatory model.

section_header: 3.2 overview of new york's climate policies — regulatory action

individual.policy_statement: Implement Advanced Clean Trucks regulation (DEC 6 NYCRR Part 218) adopting California's zero-emission vehicle standards for medium- and heavy-duty trucks and requiring large-entity reporting for owners/operators of medium- and heavy-duty trucks.
individual.verbatim_text: Advanced Clean Trucks (DEC 6 NYCRR Part 218 Regulation), adopted in 2021, incorporated California's Advanced Clean Truck zero-emission vehicle standards for MHD trucks as well as large entity reporting requirements for owners and operators of MHD trucks...
individual.section_header: 3.2 Overview of New York's Climate Policies — Regulatory Action
individual.extraction_rationale: Named adopted regulation with specified standards and reporting requirements.

section_header: 3.2 overview of new york's climate policies — regulatory action

individual.policy_statement: Implement oil and natural gas sector regulations (DEC 6 NYCRR Part 203) to lower methane and VOC emissions from New York's oil and natural gas sector.
individual.verbatim_text: Oil and Natural Gas Sector regulations (DEC 6 NYCRR Part 203 Regulations), adopted and effective in 2022 to support the goals and requirements of the Climate Act, lowers methane and volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions for sources in New York's oil and natural gas sector.
individual.section_header: 3.2 Overview of New York's Climate Policies — Regulatory Action
individual.extraction_rationale: Adopted regulation with explicit emissions reduction objective for specific pollutants.

section_header: 3.2 overview of new york's climate policies — regulatory action

individual.policy_statement: Implement Hydrofluorocarbon Standards and Reporting regulation (DEC 6 NYCRR Part 494) prohibiting specific HFC uses where safer alternatives are available, and update Part 494 to reflect Scoping Plan recommendations.
individual.verbatim_text: Hydrofluorocarbon Standards and Reporting (DEC 6 NYCRR Part 494 Regulations) prohibits specific HFCs... in certain refrigerants, aerosol propellants, and foam-blowing agent end uses that represent avoidable HFC emissions where safer alternatives are available. In 2022, DEC began pre-proposal stakeholder outreach to update Part 494 to reflect the Scoping Plan recommendations.
individual.section_header: 3.2 Overview of New York's Climate Policies — Regulatory Action
individual.extraction_rationale: Existing prohibition plus stated update process to align with plan recommendations.

section_header: 3.2 overview of new york's climate policies — regulatory action

individual.policy_statement: Implement Food Donation and Food Scraps Recycling regulation (DEC 6 NYCRR Part 350) requiring large generators to donate excess edible food and recycle remaining food scraps when within 25 miles of an organics recycler to reduce methane and VOC emissions.
individual.verbatim_text: Food Donation and Food Scraps Recycling (DEC 6 NYCRR Part 350 Regulations), adopted in 2022... requires large generators of food scraps to donate excess edible food and to recycle all remaining food scraps if they are located within 25 miles of an organics recycler, which will reduce methane and VOC emissions...
individual.section_header: 3.2 Overview of New York's Climate Policies — Regulatory Action
individual.extraction_rationale: Adopted regulation with clear requirements and eligibility condition (within 25 miles).

section_header: 3.2 overview of new york's climate policies — legislation

individual.policy_statement: Implement a two-year moratorium (Chapter 628 of the Laws of 2022) on DEC issuance of certain permits for cryptocurrency mining operations using behind-the-meter electricity and proof-of-work methods, and require DEC (with DPS) to prepare a generic environmental impact statement on this type of operation.
individual.verbatim_text: Chapter 628 of the Laws of 2022 established a two-year moratorium on the issuance of certain permits by DEC for cryptocurrency mining operations that use behind-the-meter electricity and proof-of-work authentication methods... This law directs DEC, in consultation with the New York State Department of Public Service (DPS), to prepare a generic environmental impact statement on this type of cryptocurrency mining operation.
individual.section_header: 3.2 Overview of New York's Climate Policies — Legislation
individual.extraction_rationale: Legislated moratorium plus mandated environmental review—clear binding measures.

section_header: 3.2 overview of new york's climate policies — legislation

individual.policy_statement: Implement the Utility Thermal Energy Network and Jobs Act (Chapter 375 of the Laws of 2022) requiring PSC to develop a regulatory structure for utility thermal energy networks and direct utilities to launch pilot projects in their service territories.
individual.verbatim_text: Chapter 375 of the Laws of 2022 (Utility Thermal Energy Network and Jobs Act) requires the PSC to develop a regulatory structure for utility thermal energy networks, including district geothermal and other community-scale thermal infrastructure projects, for heating and cooling homes and to direct utilities to launch pilot projects in their service territories.
individual.section_header: 3.2 Overview of New York's Climate Policies — Legislation
individual.extraction_rationale: Specific enacted law creating regulatory requirements and directing pilots.

section_header: 3.2 overview of new york's climate policies — legislation

individual.policy_statement: Advance the Accelerated Renewable Energy Growth and Community Benefit Act (Chapter 58 of the Laws of 2020) by operating the Office of Renewable Energy Siting (ORES) to streamline environmentally responsible, cost-effective siting of large-scale renewable energy projects and accelerate progress toward Climate Act clean electricity mandates.
individual.verbatim_text: Chapter 58 of the Laws of 2020 established the Accelerated Renewable Energy Growth and Community Benefit Act... This law created a first-in-the-nation Office of Renewable Energy Siting (ORES) to improve and streamline the process for the environmentally responsible and cost-effective siting of large-scale renewable energy projects across New York... will accelerate progress toward New York's nation-leading clean energy and climate mitigation requirements, including the mandate to obtain 70% of the State's electricity from renewable sources...
individual.section_header: 3.2 Overview of New York's Climate Policies — Legislation
individual.extraction_rationale: Named enacted law and institutional mechanism (ORES) with explicit purpose.

section_header: 3.2 overview of new york's climate policies — legislation

individual.policy_statement: Implement congestion pricing under the MTA Reform & Traffic Mobility Act (Chapter 59 of the Laws of 2019): a toll program for vehicles entering or remaining in Manhattan's Central Business District to reduce congestion and enhance mobility.
individual.verbatim_text: Chapter 59 of the Laws of 2019 established the Metropolitan Transportation Authority (MTA) Reform & Traffic Mobility Act (Congestion Pricing) that directs MTA to design, develop, build, and run a toll program that applies to vehicles that enter or remain in Manhattan's Central Business District. The purpose of the program is to reduce congestion and enhance mobility in Manhattan's Central Business District.
individual.section_header: 3.2 Overview of New York's Climate Policies — Legislation
individual.extraction_rationale: Legislated directive with a clearly described instrument (tolling) and purpose.

section_header: 3.2 overview of new york's climate policies — regulatory action

individual.policy_statement: Implement the Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative (RGGI) cap-and-invest emissions trading program for CO2 from electric generation, with a declining cap over time, through state regulations.
individual.verbatim_text: Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative (RGGI; DEC 6 NYCRR Part 242 Regulations and NYSERDA 21 NYCRR Part 507) is the first mandatory market-based emissions trading program... New York and other RGGI-participating states set a cap for total emissions of CO2 from electric generation facilities... The RGGI cap declines over time, gradually lowering CO2 emission limits.
individual.section_header: 3.2 Overview of New York's Climate Policies — Regulatory Action
individual.extraction_rationale: Existing binding market-based emissions policy with explicit mechanism and declining cap.

section_header: key milestones and implementation steps to-date

individual.policy_statement: DEC promulgated 6 NYCRR Part 496 establishing statewide GHG emission limits: 2030 limit equal to 60% of 1990 levels and 2050 limit equal to 15% of 1990 levels, and will publish annual statewide GHG emissions reports updating the inventory.
individual.verbatim_text:
DEC promulgated 6 NYCRR Part 496 that established the two statewide GHG emission limits called for in the Climate Act: a limit for 2030 that is equal to 60% of 1990 GHG emission levels and a limit for 2050 that is equal to 15% of 1990 emission levels.

DEC, in consultation with NYSERDA, continues to update the inventory of GHGs and will publish an annual statewide GHG emissions report that reflects these updates.
individual.section_header: Key Milestones and Implementation Steps To-Date
individual.extraction_rationale: Promulgated regulation with quantified limits plus an ongoing reporting commitment.

section_header: key milestones and implementation steps to-date

individual.policy_statement: Establish and use New York State 'Value of Carbon' guidance (DEC, with NYSERDA) to value avoided GHG emissions for State agency decision-making, recommending use of a 2% central discount rate (reported alongside 1% and 3%) and providing pollutant-specific values, updated in 2022 to include HFCs.
individual.verbatim_text:
DEC, in consultation with NYSERDA, established the Value of Carbon guidance to help State agency decision-making by placing a monetary value for the avoided emissions of GHGs.

The guidance... recommends the use of a central discount rate of 2%, which should be reported alongside a 1% and 3% discount rate... DEC updated the Value of Carbon guidance in 2022 to add values for hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs)...
individual.section_header: Key Milestones and Implementation Steps To-Date
individual.extraction_rationale: Formal cross-agency guidance intended for broad application in decision-making; includes explicit methodological requirements.

section_header: 1.3 economic opportunities and a just transition / office of just transition and worker support and community assurance fund

individual.policy_statement: Create a new Office of Just Transition and a Worker Support and Community Assurance Fund to coordinate funding and support for host communities, existing workers, and new workers during the Climate Act transition, including extending and increasing funding for the Electric Generation Facility Cessation Mitigation Program beyond 2025.
individual.verbatim_text:
Create a new Office of Just Transition and a Worker Support and Community Assurance Fund to guide ongoing program and policy support...

That apparatus should take the form of a newly created, dedicated state office charged with realizing a just transition and resourced with an accompanying fund...

New York should enact legislation to expand and extend the Electric Generation Facility Cessation Mitigation Program... The program is currently scheduled to close to new applicants on July 1, 2025. The program should be extended beyond 2025 and increased with new funding...
individual.section_header: 1.3 Economic Opportunities and a Just Transition / Office of Just Transition and Worker Support and Community Assurance Fund
individual.extraction_rationale: Explicit actionable recommendation to create an office and fund, plus specific recommendation to extend/increase an existing mitigation program.

section_header: ## t1. light-duty zero-emission vehicle adoption

individual.policy_statement: DEC should adopt California's Advanced Clean Cars 2 regulation requiring 100% of new light-duty vehicle sales to be zero-emission by 2035.
individual.verbatim_text: “Adopt California's Advanced Clean Cars 2 Regulation: Chapter 423 of the Laws of 2021 commits 100% of all new, light-duty on-road vehicle sales in New York to be ZEVs by 2035, and directs DEC to develop and propose regulations, to the extent consistent with federal law, such as Advanced Clean Cars 2. DEC should adopt the Advanced Clean Cars 2 regulation that requires 100% light-duty ZEV sales by 2035, now that it has been finalized by California.”
individual.section_header: ## T1. Light-Duty Zero-Emission Vehicle Adoption
individual.extraction_rationale: Specific regulatory action (adopt a named regulation) with a clear requirement (100% ZEV sales by 2035). Not listed as such in prior summary.

section_header: ## t1. light-duty zero-emission vehicle adoption

individual.policy_statement: New York should enact legislation to establish a dynamic, revenue-neutral 'feebate' program that provides rebates for ZEV purchases funded by fees on fossil-fuel vehicle purchases, with higher rebates targeted to LMI customers and additional rebates for used ZEVs for LMI customers.
individual.verbatim_text: “Provide enhanced ZEV purchase incentives: ... New York should enact legislation to establish a 'feebate' program that would offer direct rebates for ZEV purchases supported by imposing a fee on purchases of fossil fuel vehicles. The fee and rebate levels should be dynamic in response to market conditions and ambition levels. Such a program can be designed to be revenue-neutral and can incorporate other policy goals, such as higher rebates for LMI customers... Feebates should be applied to new car sales, but there should be an additional rebate for used ZEVs targeted toward LMI customers...”
individual.section_header: ## T1. Light-Duty Zero-Emission Vehicle Adoption
individual.extraction_rationale: Legislative program proposal with defined mechanism (feebate), funding source (fees), and equity targeting.

section_header: ## t1. light-duty zero-emission vehicle adoption

individual.policy_statement: New York should enact legislation to expand direct-to-consumer sales of ZEVs by manufacturers, provide dealer incentives for franchise dealers to sell ZEVs, and fund consumer engagement to increase ZEV interest.
individual.verbatim_text: “Enhance ZEV awareness and reduce sales barriers: New York should enact legislation to expand direct-to-consumer sales of ZEVs by manufacturers... the State should provide dealer incentives for franchise car dealers to sell ZEVs; and NYSERDA should partner with industry participants and stakeholders to fund consumer engagement activities to increase consumer interest in ZEVs.”
individual.section_header: ## T1. Light-Duty Zero-Emission Vehicle Adoption
individual.extraction_rationale: Concrete set of actions (legislation + incentives + funded engagement) to remove sales barriers.

section_header: ## t1. light-duty zero-emission vehicle adoption

individual.policy_statement: New York should fund rebates or investments for EV charging stations and hydrogen fueling stations and incorporate EV charging requirements into building codes to ensure new construction is EV-ready.
individual.verbatim_text: “Invest in and remove barriers for ZEV charging and fueling infrastructure: ... New York should fund rebates or investment in EV charging stations and hydrogen filling stations... Through the National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure formula program, DOT will identify opportunities... DOS should incorporate EV charging into building codes to ensure new construction is EV-ready.”
individual.section_header: ## T1. Light-Duty Zero-Emission Vehicle Adoption
individual.extraction_rationale: Specific interventions: funding for infrastructure plus a code-related action (EV-ready requirements).

section_header: ## t1. light-duty zero-emission vehicle adoption

individual.policy_statement: PSC should direct utilities to implement rate designs/programs that encourage off-peak or managed EV charging, examine demand-charge structures for cost-based non-discriminatory changes, and consider policies to enable vehicle-to-grid/grid-interactive value for ZEV owners.
individual.verbatim_text: “Enact utility rate design changes: The New York State Public Service Commission (PSC) should direct utilities, as appropriate, to implement programs that offer lower rates for or otherwise encourage off-peak charging and/or controlled, managed charging.... the PSC should determine whether a change is necessary in the structure of demand chargers that is cost-based and nondiscriminatory. The PSC and NYSERDA should also consider how to maximize the value of ZEVs as grid-interactive assets and storage devices...”
individual.section_header: ## T1. Light-Duty Zero-Emission Vehicle Adoption
individual.extraction_rationale: Regulatory directives and program changes specified for the PSC and utilities.

section_header: ## t2. adoption of zero-emission trucks, buses, and non-road equipment

individual.policy_statement: State agencies/authorities shall adopt and periodically revise procurement targets with funding to operationalize a zero-emission State fleet of MHD vehicles, where technically feasible, by 2040 (Executive Order 22 commitment).
individual.verbatim_text: “State fleet: In accordance with Executive Order 22, each state agency/authority shall adopt and periodically revise procurement targets, with appropriate funding allocated, to operationalize the State's November 2021 commitment to a zero-emission State fleet of MHD vehicles, where technically feasible, by 2040.”
individual.section_header: ## T2. Adoption of Zero-Emission Trucks, Buses, and Non-Road Equipment
individual.extraction_rationale: Specific government procurement mandate with date and feasibility qualifier.

section_header: ## t2. adoption of zero-emission trucks, buses, and non-road equipment

individual.policy_statement: DEC should adopt components of California's Advanced Clean Fleets regulations (or equivalent), including: (1) a drayage truck ZEV transition rule; (2) requirements for federal and 'high priority' fleets to transition to ZEVs; and (3) a requirement for 100% MHD ZEV sales by 2040, with potential targeting/exemptions for smaller fleets.
individual.verbatim_text: “Adopt additional Clean Fleets regulations: ... DEC should also adopt certain components of California's proposed Advanced Clean Fleets regulations, or equivalent measures. Specifically, DEC should adopt (1) the drayage truck regulation... (2) the regulation requiring federal and 'high priority' fleets to transition to ZEVs; and (3) the requirement for 100% MHD ZEV sales by 2040.”
individual.section_header: ## T2. Adoption of Zero-Emission Trucks, Buses, and Non-Road Equipment
individual.extraction_rationale: Specific regulatory adoption actions and an explicit 2040 sales requirement.

section_header: ## t2. adoption of zero-emission trucks, buses, and non-road equipment

individual.policy_statement: New York should fund direct incentives for purchase of zero-emission trucks, buses, and non-road equipment, prioritizing fleets in LMI/overburdened communities, small fleets, school buses, and equipment such as ports/airports/construction/farm/forestry equipment; and provide incentives/buybacks for small-engine equipment and encourage local electrification requirements.
individual.verbatim_text: “Provide enhanced ZEV purchase incentives: ... The State should fund direct incentives supporting the purchase of zero-emission trucks and buses, with a focus on fleets operating in LMI and overburdened communities, small fleets, and school buses, as well as non-road vehicles and equipment such as airport ground support equipment, port cargo handling equipment, construction, farm, and forestry equipment. The State should also provide incentives or offer buybacks for equipment with small engines...”
individual.section_header: ## T2. Adoption of Zero-Emission Trucks, Buses, and Non-Road Equipment
individual.extraction_rationale: Resource allocation (incentives/buybacks) with defined target beneficiaries and equipment categories.

section_header: ## t2. adoption of zero-emission trucks, buses, and non-road equipment

individual.policy_statement: New York should enact legislation establishing procurement/contracting rules to increase use of zero-emission equipment and vehicles on State-funded projects (including contractors/subcontractors), and DEC should adopt regulations requiring trucks accessing certain facilities (e.g., ports/intermodal railyards) to be ZEVs by a set date.
individual.verbatim_text: “Require use of ZEV equipment by State contractors and at targeted facilities: To further encourage ZEV adoption, New York should enact legislation that establishes procurement and contracting rules to increase the percentage of zero-emission equipment and vehicles used for State-funded projects, including contractors and subcontractors... DEC should also adopt regulations similar to California's Advanced Clean Fleets proposal that require MHD trucks in use at, or accessing, certain types of facilities such as ports or intermodal railyards to be ZEVs by a set date.”
individual.section_header: ## T2. Adoption of Zero-Emission Trucks, Buses, and Non-Road Equipment
individual.extraction_rationale: Two concrete policy levers: procurement legislation and facility-access regulation for ZEVs.

section_header: ## t3. community-based service enhancements

individual.policy_statement: The State should identify, plan, and implement context-specific public transportation service enhancements through community-based discussions and strengthen New York's rail network by working with federal partners to improve intercity passenger rail reliability and freight rail.
individual.verbatim_text: “Identify, plan, and implement service enhancements and offer additional shared mobility and micro-transit services: The State should work with communities and service providers to design strategies that increase utilization of existing and new public transportation options and alternatives.... Strengthen New York's rail network: The State should work with federal partners to improve the reliability of intercity passenger rail service and strengthen the freight rail system.”
individual.section_header: ## T3. Community-Based Service Enhancements
individual.extraction_rationale: Action commitments to design and implement enhancements and rail improvements; not merely descriptive.

section_header: ## b1. adopt advanced codes for highly efficient, zero-emission, and resilient new construction

individual.policy_statement: DOS and the Code Council should adopt State codes by 2025 prohibiting fossil-fuel combustion systems/equipment in new single-family and low-rise multifamily buildings (≤3 stories), and by 2028 prohibit fossil-fuel combustion systems/equipment in new commercial and larger multifamily buildings (≥4 stories).
individual.verbatim_text:
“2025: Adopt State codes that prohibit building systems or equipment used for the combustion of fossil fuels in new construction statewide of single-family and low-rise multifamily residential buildings having three stories or less... These requirements should apply to construction of buildings... that file for a building permit starting in 2025.
- 2028: Adopt State codes that prohibit building systems or equipment used for the combustion of fossil fuels in new construction statewide of multifamily residential buildings having four stories or more and commercial buildings... that file for a building permit starting in 2028.”
individual.section_header: ## B1. Adopt Advanced Codes for Highly Efficient, Zero-Emission, and Resilient New Construction
individual.extraction_rationale: Specific, dated statewide code prohibitions with applicability thresholds and permit-effective dates.

section_header: ## b3. require energy benchmarking and disclosure

individual.policy_statement: By 2024, commence a statewide energy benchmarking and disclosure program requiring owners of multifamily and commercial properties >10,000 sq ft to annually report whole-building energy and water data to NYSERDA for public disclosure, and require utilities to provide automatic aggregated whole-building uploads to ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager.
individual.verbatim_text: “2024: Commence a statewide energy benchmarking and disclosure program that requires owners of multifamily and commercial properties larger than 10,000 sq. ft. to annually report whole-building energy and water consumption data to NYSERDA for public disclosure.... Also, the PSC should require electric, gas, and water utilities to provide automatic aggregated whole-building uploads of utility customer data directly to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency's (EPA) ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager.”
individual.section_header: ## B3. Require Energy Benchmarking and Disclosure
individual.extraction_rationale: Clear statewide requirement with threshold and annual reporting plus a PSC utility data-upload requirement.

section_header: ## b3. require energy benchmarking and disclosure

individual.policy_statement: By 2025, require multifamily and commercial properties >25,000 sq ft to conduct a comprehensive energy assessment at least once every 10 years and file an assessment report with NYSERDA, on a State-established cycle or when specified work requiring a permit occurs.
individual.verbatim_text: “2025: Require multifamily and commercial properties larger than 25,000 sq. ft. to undertake a comprehensive building energy assessment (audit)... at least once every 10 years... Filing an assessment report with NYSERDA would be required on a cycle established by the State or at the time that a building permit is needed for specified work...”
individual.section_header: ## B3. Require Energy Benchmarking and Disclosure
individual.extraction_rationale: Mandatory audit frequency and filing requirement with defined building size threshold.

section_header: ## b3. require energy benchmarking and disclosure

individual.policy_statement: By 2025, require public disclosure of prior-year energy consumption as part of sale or lease listings for all buildings/units/spaces; and by 2027 require single-family buildings to disclose an energy performance rating as part of sale listings.
individual.verbatim_text:
“2025: Require owners of all single-family and multifamily residential and commercial buildings to obtain and publicly disclose, as part of sale or lease listing... the prior-year energy consumption... (at least 12 consecutive months of energy bill data).
- 2027: Require owners of single-family buildings to obtain and disclose an energy performance rating... as part of sale listing.”
individual.section_header: ## B3. Require Energy Benchmarking and Disclosure
individual.extraction_rationale: Explicit disclosure mandates with dates and scope.

section_header: ## b2. adopt standards for zero-emission equipment and the energy performance of existing buildings

individual.policy_statement: By 2030, adopt zero-emission standards prohibiting end-of-life replacement of residential-sized fossil-fuel combustion equipment for heating/cooling and hot water; by 2035 prohibit replacements of large/commercial fossil-fuel combustion equipment for heating/cooling/hot water and prohibit replacements of fossil-fuel cooking and clothes-drying appliances.
individual.verbatim_text:
“- 2030: Adopt zero-emission standards that prohibit replacements (at end of useful life) of residential-sized equipment used for the combustion of fossil fuels for heating and cooling and hot water....
- 2035: Adopt zero-emission standards that prohibit replacements (at end of useful life) of large and commercial-sized equipment used for the combustion of fossil fuels for heating, cooling, and hot water....
- 2035: Adopt zero-emission standards that prohibit replacements (at end of useful life) of fossil fuel appliances for cooking and clothes drying.”
individual.section_header: ## B2. Adopt Standards for Zero-Emission Equipment and the Energy Performance of Existing Buildings
individual.extraction_rationale: Clear prohibitions with phased dates and equipment sizing categories.

section_header: ## b2. adopt standards for zero-emission equipment and the energy performance of existing buildings

individual.policy_statement: By 2035, DEC should adopt and enforce zero-emission standards prohibiting fossil fuel use in large fuel burning equipment for heating and domestic hot water for equipment serving buildings typically 50,000 sq ft or more, requiring early retirement.
individual.verbatim_text: “2035: DEC should adopt zero-emission standards that prohibit fossil fuel use in large fuel burning equipment for heating and domestic hot water. The standards should be enforced under a new emissions enforcement regime of large combustion equipment sized to typically heat buildings 50,000 sq. ft. or more in floor area, thereby requiring early retirement.”
individual.section_header: ## B2. Adopt Standards for Zero-Emission Equipment and the Energy Performance of Existing Buildings
individual.extraction_rationale: Regulatory standard with enforcement regime, size threshold, and early-retirement implication.

section_header: components of the strategy

individual.policy_statement: Expand dedicated direct incentives and financial support mechanisms for weatherization, energy efficiency, and electrification for LMI households, affordable housing, public housing, and Disadvantaged Communities, including partnerships, streamlined enrollment, and people-centered funding across governments.
individual.verbatim_text: - Prioritize LMI households, affordable housing, and Disadvantaged Communities: The State should expand dedicated direct incentives and financial support mechanisms for weatherization, energy efficiency, and electrification for LMI households, affordable housing, public housing, and Disadvantaged Communities. This includes developing new partnerships to effectively deliver programs (such as through housing agencies, community development financial institutions, and local community-based organizations), streamlining program enrollment models, and establishing people-centered policies, programs, and funding across local, State, and federal governments...
individual.section_header: Components of the Strategy
individual.extraction_rationale: Concrete commitment using 'should expand' plus specific implementation actions (partnerships, streamlining enrollment, etc.).

section_header: components of the strategy

individual.policy_statement: Prioritize State facilities in Disadvantaged Communities for energy efficiency and electrification upgrades pursuant to Executive Order 22.
individual.verbatim_text: Pursuant to Executive Order 22, State entities should prioritize State facilities located in Disadvantaged Communities for energy efficiency and electrification upgrades, which will lower environmental impacts on these communities.
individual.section_header: Components of the Strategy
individual.extraction_rationale: Explicit directive tied to an executive order; clear prioritization action.

section_header: components of the strategy

individual.policy_statement: Support and accelerate efficiency, electrification, and resilience improvements in public housing and pilot resilience solutions (including resilience hubs with backup power) leveraging federal and other funding.
individual.verbatim_text: - Prioritize energy upgrades and pilot new resilience strategies in public housing: The State should support and accelerate efficiency, electrification, and resilience in public housing... The State should pilot a range of resilience solutions in public housing... Specifically, the State should support resilience centers (or resilience hubs) in public housing developments... Such centers should include backup power (including solar-storage pilots) for multi-day outages...
individual.section_header: Components of the Strategy
individual.extraction_rationale: Specific intervention (public housing upgrades, pilots, resilience hubs) with defined features.

section_header: components of the strategy

individual.policy_statement: Implement the Solar Energy Equity Framework strategy for community solar that provides bill savings to income-eligible households and/or benefits affordable housing or public buildings in Disadvantaged Communities, with rules directing benefits to low-income residents.
individual.verbatim_text: - Expand access to community solar: Under the State's Solar Energy Equity Framework, NYSERDA will implement a strategy for community solar projects that provide electric bill savings to income-eligible households and/or that benefit affordable housing or public buildings in Disadvantaged Communities, with program rules that direct benefits to low-income residents.
individual.section_header: Components of the Strategy
individual.extraction_rationale: Clear implementing agency (NYSERDA) and defined program outcome (bill savings) and targeting.

section_header: components of the strategy

individual.policy_statement: Create a new Retrofit and Electrification Readiness Fund to pay for necessary non-energy building improvements (and broadband installation costs when funding energy projects) to enable energy measures for specified priority buildings/households.
individual.verbatim_text: - Fund non-energy improvements when necessary: ... The State should create a new Retrofit and Electrification Readiness Fund for LMI households, affordable housing, rent regulated housing, public housing, and residential buildings in Disadvantaged Communities to cover costs of non-energy building improvements that are necessary to install energy measures and broadband installation costs when funding energy projects.
individual.section_header: Components of the Strategy
individual.extraction_rationale: Resource allocation via creation of a named fund with defined eligible uses/beneficiaries.

section_header: b5. expand access to public and private low-cost financing

individual.policy_statement: Help address lender barriers via lender education/outreach and tools, and continue convening the financial industry to bring capital to building decarbonization in compliance with the Climate Act.
individual.verbatim_text: New York should help to address this barrier through lender education and outreach, and by making available case studies and modeling tools so that lenders can appropriately underwrite to energy performance standards and applicable regulatory requirements. NYSERDA and HCR plan to continue to convene the financial industry to explore ways to bring capital to building decarbonization in compliance with the Climate Act.
individual.section_header: B5. Expand Access to Public and Private Low-Cost Financing
individual.extraction_rationale: Specific interventions (education/outreach, tools, convenings) with responsible entities.

section_header: b7. align energy price signals with policy goals

individual.policy_statement: Expand low-income utility bill discount programs and ensure assistance programs adjust for increased cooling needs and electrified heating transitions.
individual.verbatim_text: - Align bill discount programs: For low-income households, the State should expand existing low-income energy bill discount programs administered by the major electric and gas utilities and ensure that current or future public utility bill assistance programs recognize and adjust for both increased cooling needs and the shift from traditional forms of heating to efficient electrification.
individual.section_header: B7. Align Energy Price Signals with Policy Goals
individual.extraction_rationale: Concrete expansion commitment for specific program type (bill discounts) with defined adjustments.
